{"author": null, "closed_at": null, "collaborating_teams": null, "collaborating_users": null, "created_at": null, "credits": [{"login": "Kwstubbs", "type": "reporter"}], "credits_detailed": [{"state": "accepted", "type": "reporter", "user": {"avatar_url": "https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/11400619?v=4", "events_url": "https://api.github.com/users/Kwstubbs/events{/privacy}", "followers_url": "https://api.github.com/users/Kwstubbs/followers", "following_url": "https://api.github.com/users/Kwstubbs/following{/other_user}", "gists_url": "https://api.github.com/users/Kwstubbs/gists{/gist_id}", "gravatar_id": "", "html_url": "https://github.com/Kwstubbs", "id": 11400619, "login": "Kwstubbs", "node_id": "MDQ6VXNlcjExNDAwNjE5", "organizations_url": "https://api.github.com/users/Kwstubbs/orgs", "received_events_url": "https://api.github.com/users/Kwstubbs/received_events", "repos_url": "https://api.github.com/users/Kwstubbs/repos", "site_admin": true, "starred_url": "https://api.github.com/users/Kwstubbs/starred{/owner}{/repo}", "subscriptions_url": "https://api.github.com/users/Kwstubbs/subscriptions", "type": "User", "url": "https://api.github.com/users/Kwstubbs", "user_view_type": "public"}}], "cve_id": "CVE-2026-44698", "cvss": {"score": 8.3, "vector_string": "CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:H/PR:N/UI:R/S:C/C:H/I:H/A:H"}, "cvss_severities": {"cvss_v3": {"score": 8.3, "vector_string": "CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:H/PR:N/UI:R/S:C/C:H/I:H/A:H"}, "cvss_v4": {"score": null, "vector_string": null}}, "cwe_ids": ["CWE-94", "CWE-346", "CWE-749", "CWE-940"], "cwes": [{"cwe_id": "CWE-94", "name": "Improper Control of Generation of Code ('Code Injection')"}, {"cwe_id": "CWE-346", "name": "Origin Validation Error"}, {"cwe_id": "CWE-749", "name": "Exposed Dangerous Method or Function"}, {"cwe_id": "CWE-940", "name": "Improper Verification of Source of a Communication Channel"}], "description": "## Summary

The Home Assistant Companion apps for [Android](https://github.com/home-assistant/android/) and [iOS](https://github.com/home-assistant/iOS/) expose a JavaScript bridge to the in-app WebView `window.externalApp` on Android and `webkit.messageHandlers.getExternalAuth` (alongside `revokeExternalAuth` and `externalBus`) on iOS. Two flaws expose the bridge to all frames (including cross-origin iframes) and unsanitized interpolation of the JavaScript callback identifier allows a cross-origin iframe rendered inside the Companion app to execute arbitrary JavaScript in the Home Assistant frontend's main-frame origin and exfiltrate the signed-in user's access token.

This issue was reported by the GitHub Security Lab as `GHSL-2026-104`.

## Severity

**High.** A cross-origin iframe loaded by the victim's dashboard can obtain the signed-in user's Home Assistant access token without any further interaction, granting full REST API access for the token's lifetime.

## Affected versions

| Platform | Affected                         | Fixed      |
| --- |----------------------------------|------------|
| Home Assistant Companion for Android | All versions prior to `2026.4.4` | `2026.4.4` |
| Home Assistant Companion for iOS | All versions prior to `2026.4.1` | `2026.4.1` |

## Details

The Companion apps register a native bridge with the WebView — `window.externalApp` on Android (via `addJavascriptInterface()`) and `webkit.messageHandlers.getExternalAuth` / `revokeExternalAuth` / `externalBus` on iOS (via `WKUserContentController.add(_:name:)`) — so the Home Assistant frontend can call native functionality such as `getExternalAuth` and `revokeExternalAuth`. Two issues combine to make this bridge dangerous:

1. **Bridge exposed to all frames.** On Android, `addJavascriptInterface()` injects the object into the JavaScript global of *every* frame in the WebView, including cross-origin iframes; on iOS, the `SafeScriptMessageHandler` did not check `WKFrameInfo.isMainFrame` and forwarded messages from any frame including cross-origin iframes to the delegate. The apps perform no origin check inside the handlers.
2. **Unsanitized callback injection.** The `callback` field of the JSON payload passed to `getExternalAuth` (and `revokeExternalAuth`) is interpolated verbatim into a JavaScript string of the form `<callback>(true, <auth-json>)`, which is then passed to `WebView.evaluateJavascript()` on Android and `WKWebView.evaluateJavaScript(_:)` on iOS. There is no allowlist, regex check, or escaping at any point.

`evaluateJavascript()` always runs in the **main frame's** JavaScript context. By supplying an IIFE-shaped expression as the `callback` value, a cross-origin iframe causes attacker-controlled code to execute **in the Home Assistant frontend's origin**, with the access token passed in as a function argument.

A realistic vector is the built-in [Webpage card](https://www.home-assistant.io/dashboards/iframe/), which renders an arbitrary URL as a cross-origin `<iframe>` inside the dashboard. Any user who has added a Webpage card pointing to a third-party URL a weather widget, a Windy embed, a status page, an externally-hosted dashboard is exposed if that third party serves, or is later compromised to serve, malicious JavaScript.

The sibling endpoint `revokeExternalAuth` performs its destructive operations (`revokeSession()` and removing the server entry) *before* any callback handling. A cross-origin iframe calling it can revoke the victim's refresh token server-side and delete the server from the app, forcing the user back through onboarding a one-shot denial of service that does not depend on the token-exfiltration path.

## Impact

This issue may lead to **information disclosure** (Home Assistant access token) and **smart-home compromise**.

The stolen `access_token` is a standard OAuth 2.0 bearer token bound to whichever Home Assistant user is signed into the Companion app on the device. While the token is valid, the attacker gains:

- Full Home Assistant REST API access for the token's lifetime.
- Service-call capability control of any device, automation, or script the user can control.
- Read access to all entity states.
- Management of automations, scripts, and users.

Additionally, `revokeExternalAuth` provides an unauthenticated denial of service: a cross-origin iframe can revoke the victim's refresh token and delete the server entry from the app, forcing re-onboarding.

## Attack scenario

1. The victim has the Home Assistant Companion app installed and is signed in to a Home Assistant server.
2. The victim has added a Webpage (iframe) card to a dashboard pointing to a third-party URL the attacker controls directly, or via a later compromise of that third party.
3. The victim opens the dashboard containing the Webpage card in the Companion app.
4. The cross-origin iframe detects the `externalApp` bridge on `window` and calls `getExternalAuth` with a JSON payload whose `callback` field is an IIFE that beacons its argument out to an attacker-controlled host.
5. The Companion app interpolates the IIFE verbatim into a script string and runs it via `evaluateJavascript()` in the main-frame origin. The Home Assistant access token is sent to the attacker.
6. The attacker uses the bearer token against the Home Assistant REST API as the signed-in user.

## Prerequisites

- The victim is using a vulnerable version of the Companion app (see *Affected versions*).
- The victim's dashboard contains a Webpage / iframe card whose target URL is, or becomes, attacker-controlled.

## Patches

- **Home Assistant Companion for Android `2026.4.4`** fixes the issue.
- **Home Assistant Companion for iOS `2026.4.1`** fixes the issue.

The two platforms apply different but complementary mitigations.

### Android

- Introduce a new origin-aware `externalAppV2` bridge using `WebViewCompat.addWebMessageListener()` with frame and origin checks (`isMainFrame`, `sourceOrigin.hasSameOrigin(loadedUrl)`). Used when the server is on `2026.4.2` or newer and the WebView supports `WebViewFeature.WEB_MESSAGE_LISTENER`. Older servers and WebViews continue to use the legacy `externalApp` bridge, which is now safe due to the callback restriction below.
- Restrict the `callback` field of `getExternalAuth` and `revokeExternalAuth` to the two exact identifiers used by the Home Assistant frontend (`externalAuthSetToken` and `externalAuthRevokeToken`). All other values are rejected before any interpolation, and rejection in `revokeExternalAuth` happens *before* the destructive operations execute.

### iOS

- Tighten the existing `SafeScriptMessageHandler` to validate `isMainFrame` together with a full `scheme://host:port` origin match against the configured server URLs (`internalUrl` / `externalUrl` / `cloudUrl`). The previous host-only check did not catch attackers running on a different scheme or port of the same hostname.

## Workarounds

If you cannot update immediately:

- Remove any **Webpage** (iframe) cards from your dashboards.
- Avoid embedding third-party URLs (weather widgets, status pages, external dashboards) via the Webpage card until the app is updated.

## CWEs

- CWE-749: Exposed Dangerous Method or Function
- CWE-94: Improper Control of Generation of Code ('Code Injection')
- CWE-940: Improper Verification of Source of a Communication Channel
- CWE-346: Origin Validation Error

## Credit

This issue was discovered and reported by GitHub Security Lab team member [@Kwstubbs (Kevin Stubbings)](https://github.com/Kwstubbs).", "ghsa_id": "GHSA-7jp2-p2fw-mgvf", "html_url": title: Cross-origin iframe access token exfiltration via WebView JS bridge callback injection
